1: yad Adonai YHWH naflah alai ('the hand of the Lord YHWH fell upon me'): the phrase yad YHWH (hand of YHWH) marks ecstatic prophetic experience — a divine force seizing the prophet for vision or transport. Compare Ezekiel 1:3; Ezekiel 3:14, 22; Ezekiel 33:22; Ezekiel 40:1. The elders of Judah sitting before Ezekiel in exile suggests he has become a recognized spiritual authority among the exiles even before the formal destruction of Jerusalem.
2: demut-mar'eh esh ('something with the appearance of fire'): the figure that appears is identical in description to the throne-figure of chapter 1 — the deliberate verbal echo connects this vision to the divine chariot. From his loins downward: fire; from his loins upward: gleaming brightness like hashmal (electrum/amber). This is a theophanic appearance of the divine, not an angel.
3: tziur haqin'ah ('the image of jealousy'): qin'ah (jealousy/zeal) is the covenantal emotion YHWH declares in the Decalogue: 'I, YHWH your God, am a jealous God' (Ex Ezekiel 20:5). The idol placed at the gate of the altar provokes YHWH's jealousy by occupying the sacred space where only YHWH should be acknowledged. The idol is identified by the response it provokes in YHWH.
4: kevod Elohei Yisrael ('the glory of the God of Israel'): the kavod that appeared in chapter 1 at the River Chebar is now seen in Jerusalem — still present, still resident, but about to observe the abominations that will cause it to depart. The kavod's presence here sets up the drama of chapters 8-11: the glory witnesses the unfaithfulness and then withdraws.
5: miznafon la-sha'ar ha-mizbeach ('north of the altar gate'): the north was considered the direction of divine approach (cf. Job 37:22; Isaiah 14:13). An idol placed at the north gate of the altar — the most theologically charged direction — is a maximum affront to YHWH. The cardinal points of the compass become the geography of apostasy in this chapter: north (v5), inner court (v6), entrance of the outer court (v7-12), north gate of the inner court (v14), east gate of the inner court (v16).
6: lemarchaqeni me'al miqdashi ('to drive me far from my sanctuary'): YHWH frames the abominations in terms of their effect on him — they push him away from his own house. The divine presence is sensitive, not automatic; persistent covenant violation creates a moral/relational incompatibility between YHWH and the space that was dedicated to his presence. Judgment is the consequence of the people successfully driving YHWH away.
7: chayim chur bachomah ('a hole in the wall'): Ezekiel is commanded to dig through the wall — a prophetic act of investigation. The wall conceals something; what is hidden is worse than what is public. The inward/concealed nature of the abomination (the 70 elders in a secret chamber) represents the systemic nature of the apostasy: it is not just a public display but an internal practice of the leadership.
8: The command to dig (chathor-lecha) precedes the reveal — the prophet as investigator of hidden sin. The image of tunneling through a wall to see hidden chambers anticipates the revelation that follows. The progressively revealed layers of apostasy (idol at the gate, animals painted inside, women weeping, men facing east) represent a kind of tour of unfaithfulness moving from exterior to interior, from obvious to occult.
9: re'eh et-hato'evot hara'ot ('see the great abominations'): YHWH directs Ezekiel's vision — he sees what YHWH wants him to see. The tour of abominations is not Ezekiel's discovery but a divinely guided revelation of exactly how deep the apostasy runs. Each scene is observed at YHWH's prompting: 'come and see' (bo ur'eh) — v9, 13, 15, 17.
10: kol tavnit remes uvehema sheketz ('every form of creeping things and beasts and abominations'): the inner chamber is covered with murals of unclean animals — the precise categories prohibited in Leviticus 11. The 70 elders worship before images of everything that Israel is forbidden to eat. The Levitical purity system is being systematically inverted in the secret worship chamber.
11: shivim ish miziqnei beit Yisrael ('seventy men of the elders of the house of Israel'): the council of elders — 70 men as in Numbers 11:16-17 where 70 elders received the Spirit. The legitimate governance structure of Israel has been co-opted for secret idol worship. Ya'azanyahu ben Shafan: named — the son of Shaphan the scribe (2 Kings 22:3), who read the discovered Torah scroll to Josiah. The family of reform is implicated in apostasy.
12: bemachshak ish becheder maskito ('each in his room of idols/carved images'): maskiyot (carved images/rooms of imagery) — each elder has a private chamber of devotion. The corporate apostasy at the temple is matched by individual private idolatry. Their theological rationale: ein YHWH ro'eh otanu ('YHWH does not see us') — YHWH has abandoned the land. The premise for the secret worship is the claim that divine oversight has ceased.
13: The divine 'come and see' formula repeated — this is only the second of four scenes. Each scene is worse than the previous. YHWH is systematically showing Ezekiel the cumulative weight of Israel's unfaithfulness, building to the fullest possible indictment before announcing the judgment of chapters 9-11.
14: nashim yoshvot mevakot et-haTammuz ('women sitting weeping for the Tammuz'): Tammuz is the Mesopotamian god of vegetation/fertility (Sumerian Dumuzi), whose annual death and descent to the underworld was mourned by women in ritual lamentation at midsummer. The cult was explicitly Babylonian — Ezekiel's exilic audience would recognize it immediately. The north gate of the inner court is devoted to foreign fertility-cult mourning.
15: The third 'come and see' — greater abominations to come. The tour moves from animal-worship murals (Ezekiel 8:10), to Tammuz-lamentation (Ezekiel 8:14), to solar cult (Ezekiel 8:16) — roughly tracing the geography of the temple courts from outer to inner, and also the chronological history of Israel's apostasy from Canaanite (bamot) to Mesopotamian to Egyptian/solar influences.
16: esrim vachamisha ish ('twenty-five men'): the number may correspond to the 24 priestly divisions plus the high priest — the entire cultic leadership. mishtachavitim qedmah lashemesh ('bowing down to the sun'): qedem (east) — they face east, away from the Holy of Holies, toward the rising sun. This is the ultimate liturgical inversion: the worshippers of YHWH turn their backs on his dwelling to face a heavenly body.
17: shalchu et-hazmorat el-appam ('they put the branch to their nose'): a notoriously obscure gesture — possibly a ritual act in solar or fertility worship (holding a plant toward the face in salutation or offering). Some emend to 'nose' = face, others read it as a deliberate obscenity. Whatever the gesture, YHWH frames it as a provocation: they are outraging him and then returning to do more provocation. Chamasam ('their violence') — the sins accumulate.
18: lo-tachus einai velo echmol ('my eye will not spare and I will not pity'): the identical refusal of mercy from Ezekiel 7:4, 9 — but now pronounced in the context of the observed abominations. The justice of the upcoming slaughter in chapter 9 is grounded in the evidence of chapters 8: YHWH has seen everything in the secret chambers and at the east gate. veqare'u be'oznai qol gadol ('they cry in my ears with a loud voice'): even loud prayer in the coming crisis will not be heard.
